{"agent":"reviewer_1","payload":{"summary":"The paper presents a novel approach to unsupervised causal learning using variational inference with a causal prior. It aims to recover causal structures and latent variables from unstructured data, addressing limitations in existing methods that rely on structured datasets or known variables.","significance_and_novelty":"The significance of this work lies in its attempt to bridge the gap between causal inference and variational autoencoders (VAEs) in the context of unstructured data. The proposed method introduces a new framework for learning causal relationships without prior knowledge of the variables, which is a ","reasons_for_acceptance":["The paper addresses a relevant and timely topic in representation learning and causal inference, which is of high interest to the ICLR community.","The proposed method demonstrates a novel integration of variational inference with causal modeling, potentially advancing the state of the art in unsupervised learning.","The theoretical framework is well-founded, with clear definitions and assumptions laid out for the model."],"reasons_for_rejection":["The proofs provided in the paper lack sufficient rigor in some areas, particularly in the identifiability section. More detailed explanations of the assumptions and their implications are needed to strengthen the theoretical claims.","The paper could benefit from a more thorough empirical evaluation, including comparisons with existing methods on benchmark datasets to validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach."],"suggestions":["Enhance the theoretical section by providing more detailed proofs and clarifying the assumptions made, especially regarding the identifiability of the model.","Include empirical results that compare the proposed method with existing causal inference techniques and VAEs on standard datasets to demonstrate its practical utility and effectiveness.","Consider discussing the limitations of the approach and potential areas for future work to provide a more balanced view of the contributions."],"rating":6,"confidence":4},"input_message":"Analyze the paper and provide a structured review.","context_used":"Paper source: reg_237_unsupervised_learning_of_causa.pdf

2 R ELATED WORK
Our work lies on the intersection of causality, variational inference, representation learning, and
high-dimensional unstructured input domains.
Causal inference deals with determining the causes and effects from data. Causal discovery methods
generally focus on recovering the causal graph responsible for generating the observed data, e.g.
Spirtes & Glymour (1991); Chickering (2003) (for an overview of methods in see Peters et al. (2017)).
However, these methods are largely applied to structured datasets such as medical (Brooks-Gunn
et al., 1992; Sachs et al., 2005; Louizos et al., 2017) or economics data LaLonde (1986) where the
observed variables are provided by domain specialists. In contrast, we focus on unstructured data
where the variables are not provided a priori.

Our work lies on the intersection of causality, variational inference, representation learning, and
high-dimensional unstructured input domains.
Causal inference deals with determining the causes and effects from data. Causal discovery methods
generally focus on recovering the causal graph responsible for generating the observed data, e.g.
Spirtes & Glymour (1991); Chickering (2003) (for an overview of methods in see Peters et al. (2017)).
However, these methods are largely applied to structured datasets such as medical (Brooks-Gunn
et al., 1992; Sachs et al., 2005; Louizos et al., 2017) or economics data LaLonde (1986) where the
observed variables are provided by domain specialists. In contrast, we focus on unstructured data

6 C ONCLUSIONS
In this work we proposed a general model that naturally extends the variational learning framework
to learn a non-linear Structural Equation Model (SEM) as the prior, which enables causal learning to
be performed on perceptual modalities such as images and video. To reconcile the non-linearity of
SEMs while using Gaussian variables we proposed a fully differentiable method that locally linearises
the SEM to obtain a locally-Gaussian distribution. Furthermore, we relaxed the search over causal
graphs as a joint continuous optimization over non-linear causal functions f. The proposed method
shows promise to scale to high-dimensional data and moderately complex SEMs, however future
work should explore more large-scale data such as long videos and other input modalities.

In this work we proposed a general model that naturally extends the variational learning framework
to learn a non-linear Structural Equation Model (SEM) as the prior, which enables causal learning to

meta_review.summary: The paper proposes a novel approach to unsupervised causal learning using a causal-prior Variational Autoencoder (VAE), aiming to recover causal structures from unstructured data. While the reviewers acknowledge the novelty and relevance of the work, they express concerns regarding the rigor of the theoretical framework and the depth of empirical evaluation. The integration of causal inference with representation learning is seen as a significant contribution, but the lack of comprehensive comparisons with existing methods and insufficient reproducibility details limit the overall assessment.
key_points: The proposed method integrates causal inference with variational inference, addressing a relevant gap in the literature.
The theoretical framework is well-founded but lacks rigor in certain proofs, particularly in the identifiability section.
Empirical evaluations are insufficient; comparisons with existing methods are limited and reproducibility details are sparse.
The method shows promise in recovering causal structures from both atemporal and time-varying variables, indicating potential for future research.

response_payload.summary: The paper presents a novel approach to unsupervised causal learning using variational inference with a causal prior. It aims to recover causal structures and latent variables from unstructured data, addressing limitations in existing methods that rely on structured datasets or known variables.
response_payload.significance_and_novelty: The significance of this work lies in its attempt to bridge the gap between causal inference and variational autoencoders (VAEs) in the context of unstructured data. The proposed method introduces a new framework for learning causal relationships without prior knowledge of the variables, which is a
reasons_for_acceptance: The paper addresses a relevant and timely topic in representation learning and causal inference, which is of high interest to the ICLR community.
The proposed method demonstrates a novel integration of variational inference with causal modeling, potentially advancing the state of the art in unsupervised learning.
The theoretical framework is well-founded, with clear definitions and assumptions laid out for the model.
reasons_for_rejection: The proofs provided in the paper lack sufficient rigor in some areas, particularly in the identifiability section. More detailed explanations of the assumptions and their implications are needed to strengthen the theoretical claims.
The paper could benefit from a more thorough empirical evaluation, including comparisons with existing methods on benchmark datasets to validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
suggestions: Enhance the theoretical section by providing more detailed proofs and clarifying the assumptions made, especially regarding the identifiability of the model.
Include empirical results that compare the proposed method with existing causal inference techniques and VAEs on standard datasets to demonstrate its practical utility and effectiveness.
Consider discussing the limitations of the approach and potential areas for future work to provide a more balanced view of the contributions.

retrieval.base_query:
ABSTRACT
Endowing deep neural networks with the ability to reason about cause and effect
would be an important step to make them more robust and interpretable. In this
work we propose a variational framework that allows deep networks to learn
latent variables and their causal relationships from unstructured data, with no
supervision, or labeled interventions. Starting from an abstract Structural Equation
Model (SEM), we show that maximizing its posterior probability yields a similar
construction to a Variational Auto-Encoder (V AE), but with a structured prior
coupled by non-linear equations. This prior represents an interpretable SEM with
learnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. arnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. Unfortunately,
computing KL-divergences with this non-linear prior is intractable. We show
how linearizing arbitrary SEMs via back-propagation produces local non-isotropic
Gaussian priors, for which the KL-divergences can be computed efficiently and
differentiably. We propose two versions, one for IID data (such as images) which
detects related causal variables within a sample, and one for non-IID data (such
as video) which detects variables that are also related over time. Our proposal is
complementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experi lementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experiment with
recovering causal models from images, and learning temporal relations based on
the Super Mario Bros videogame.

response_payload.summary: The paper presents a novel approach to unsupervised learning of causal relationships using a causal-prior Variational Autoencoder (VAE). The authors validate their method through two experiments: one focusing on atemporal variables and the other on time-varying variables. While the proposed method shows promise in recovering causal structures, the empirical evaluation lacks depth in terms of baselines and reproducibility.
response_payload.significance_and_novelty: The significance of this work lies in its attempt to integrate causal inference into representation learning, which is a relatively underexplored area. The novelty of the causal-prior VAE is noteworthy, as it aims to disentangle causal relationships from entangled latent representations typically in
reasons_for_acceptance: The proposed causal-prior VAE shows potential in recovering causal structures from data, which is a valuable contribution to the field of representation learning.
The experiments conducted demonstrate the method's ability to learn both atemporal and temporal causal relationships, providing a foundation for future research.
reasons_for_rejection: The empirical validation is insufficient; the authors do not compare their method against a comprehensive set of baselines, which limits the assessment of its effectiveness.

The reproducibility of the experiments is not adequately addressed. Details regarding dataset preparation, hyperparameter settings, and training procedures are sparse, making it difficult for others to replicate the results.
suggestions: Include more baseline models for comparison, such as traditional VAEs, other causal inference methods, and state-of-the-art representation learning techniques to better contextualize the performance of the causal-prior VAE.
Provide detailed descriptions of the datasets used, including how they were generated and any preprocessing steps taken. This will enhance reproducibility.
Consider conducting ablation studies to understand the impact of different components of the causal-prior VAE on performance.
Clarify the evaluation metrics used to assess the performance of the models in both experiments, as this information is currently lacking.

retrieval.base_query:
ABSTRACT
Endowing deep neural networks with the ability to reason about cause and effect
would be an important step to make them more robust and interpretable. In this
work we propose a variational framework that allows deep networks to learn
latent variables and their causal relationships from unstructured data, with no
supervision, or labeled interventions. Starting from an abstract Structural Equation
Model (SEM), we show that maximizing its posterior probability yields a similar
construction to a Variational Auto-Encoder (V AE), but with a structured prior
coupled by non-linear equations. This prior represents an interpretable SEM with
learnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. arnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. Unfortunately,
computing KL-divergences with this non-linear prior is intractable. We show
how linearizing arbitrary SEMs via back-propagation produces local non-isotropic
Gaussian priors, for which the KL-divergences can be computed efficiently and
differentiably. We propose two versions, one for IID data (such as images) which
detects related causal variables within a sample, and one for non-IID data (such
as video) which detects variables that are also related over time. Our proposal is
complementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experi lementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experiment with
recovering causal models from images, and learning temporal relations based on
the Super Mario Bros videogame.

6 C ONCLUSIONS
In this work we proposed a general model that naturally extends the variational learning framework
to learn a non-linear Structural Equation Model (SEM) as the prior, which enables causal learning to

reasons_for_acceptance: The paper addresses a critical gap in the literature by applying causal learning techniques to unstructured data, which is a growing area of interest.

The empirical results show promise, indicating that the method can effectively learn latent variables with complex dependencies.

ABSTRACT
Endowing deep neural networks with the ability to reason about cause and effect
would be an important step to make them more robust and interpretable. In this
work we propose a variational framework that allows deep networks to learn
latent variables and their causal relationships from unstructured data, with no
supervision, or labeled interventions. Starting from an abstract Structural Equation
Model (SEM), we show that maximizing its posterior probability yields a similar
construction to a Variational Auto-Encoder (V AE), but with a structured prior
coupled by non-linear equations. This prior represents an interpretable SEM with
learnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. arnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. Unfortunately,
computing KL-divergences with this non-linear prior is intractable. We show
how linearizing arbitrary SEMs via back-propagation produces local non-isotropic
Gaussian priors, for which the KL-divergences can be computed efficiently and
differentiably. We propose two versions, one for IID data (such as images) which
detects related causal variables within a sample, and one for non-IID data (such
as video) which detects variables that are also related over time. Our proposal is
complementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experi lementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experiment with
recovering causal models from images, and learning temporal relations based on
the Super Mario Bros videogame.
